sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Blast injuries occur from explosions during mining operations (blasting) or from accidental detonation of explosives or flammable gases. Injuries result from four distinct mechanisms simultaneously.

**Four Mechanisms of Blast Injury**
| Type | Mechanism | Organs Affected |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Overpressure/blast wave | Lungs, ears, bowel, sinuses — air-filled organs |
| **Secondary** | Fragmentation — projectiles from explosion debris | Any body region — penetrating wounds |
| **Tertiary** | Translocation — victim thrown by blast wind | Blunt trauma, fractures, TBI |
| **Quaternary** | All other effects: flash burns, crush from collapse, toxic inhalation | Burns, inhalation injury, crush |

**Key Principle**
External injuries may dramatically **underestimate** internal injury. A patient with no visible wounds may have lethal primary blast lung injury.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Primary Blast Lung (Most Lethal Primary Blast Injury)**
- May present with NO external injuries
- Onset: immediate or **delayed up to 48 hours**
- Haemoptysis, dyspnoea, hypoxia, chest pain
- Bilateral pulmonary contusions on CXR (if available)
- Cyanosis, decreased SpO2
- High mortality if severe

**Tympanic Membrane Rupture (Most Common Primary Blast Injury)**
- Ear pain, sudden hearing loss, tinnitus
- Visible perforation on otoscopy
- Blood in ear canal
- Usually self-healing — keep dry, no ear drops
- Bilateral TM rupture = significant blast exposure → investigate for blast lung

**Blast Wave (Primary) — Other Organs**
- **Abdominal:** bowel perforation — abdominal pain, guarding, delayed peritonitis
- **Eye:** globe rupture, hyphaema — visual disturbance, eye pain
- **Sinus:** sinus fractures, bleeding

**Secondary (Fragmentation) Injuries**
- Multiple entry wounds of varying size
- May not be visible in hair, axillae, perineum — do full exposure
- Haemorrhage — may be severe and occult (abdominal)

**Tertiary (Translocation) Injuries**
- Head injury, spinal injury
- Long bone fractures, joint dislocations
- Solid organ injury (liver, spleen, kidney)

**Quaternary**
- Burns: flash (superficial/partial thickness), contact
- Crush injury from structural collapse
- Toxic inhalation (dust, chemical combustion products)

**Danger Signs — Blast Lung**
- SpO2 <94% in absence of airway obstruction
- Haemoptysis
- Bilateral chest findings
- Dyspnoea at rest

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Blast Lung — Ventilation Caution**
- **Avoid high-pressure positive pressure ventilation** where possible — barotrauma risk in injured lung
- Use lowest tidal volumes and pressures that maintain adequate SpO2
- Air embolism risk: avoid Valsalva, coughing, straining in suspected blast lung

**Do Not Be Falsely Reassured**
- Walking wounded may have serious blast lung — reassess all survivors regularly
- Initial SpO2 may be normal then deteriorate over hours

**Scene Safety**
- Confirm with mine management that the area is secured — secondary explosion risk from residual explosive or ignition sources
- Multiple casualties likely — activate MCI protocol early

**Ear Protection and Communication**
- Survivors may have significant hearing loss — use visual communication, written instructions if needed

**Toxic Inhalation**
- Explosives combustion produces nitrogen oxides, carbon monoxide, and fine particulates
- All patients near blast should receive supplemental oxygen
- See toxic-inhalations guideline if persistent respiratory symptoms

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Scene Assessment**
1. Confirm scene safety with mine management — no secondary blast risk
2. Activate site MCI plan if multiple casualties
3. Call RFDS early — blast casualties require hospital care

**Triage**
- All blast casualties must be assessed — including apparently uninjured bystanders
- Re-triage regularly — blast lung can deteriorate over hours

**Primary Survey — All Blast Casualties**
- **Airway:** open, suction, protect — blast lung patients tolerate airway manoeuvres poorly
- **Breathing:** SpO2 (critical marker), respiratory rate, bilateral air entry — treat hypoxia urgently
- **Circulation:** control external haemorrhage (tourniquets, pressure), two large-bore IV, TXA 1 g IV if haemorrhagic
- **Disability:** GCS, pupils — high rate of TBI from translocation
- **Exposure:** full body inspection for fragmentation wounds, burns

**Blast Lung Management**
- Oxygen: high-flow 15 L/min NRB; titrate to SpO2 >94%
- Position: sitting upright if haemodynamically stable
- Avoid BVM with high pressures if spontaneously breathing — support rather than override
- IV access, fluid resuscitation cautiously (avoid fluid overload in lung injury)
- Analgesia: morphine IV titrated — avoid IM in haemodynamic instability

**TM Rupture**
- Confirm no other head/ear injury
- Keep ear dry — no ear drops, no cotton wool
- Cover with clean dressing
- Routine ENT follow-up — most heal spontaneously within 8 weeks

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**Blast Lung — Deteriorating Patient**
- NIPPV (CPAP) may be used cautiously if patient is cooperative and haemodynamically stable
- Intubation if unconscious or apnoeic — lowest pressures possible, permissive hypoxia acceptable over barotrauma
- Tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight
- Avoid PEEP >8 cmH2O if possible
- Suction haemoptysis, maintain airway clear

**Pneumothorax**
- High rate post-blast
- Tension pneumothorax: needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL), finger thoracostomy if trained
- Bilateral pneumothorax possible with primary blast — consider bilateral decompression if deteriorating

**Haemorrhage Control**
- Tourniquet for limb haemorrhage
- Haemostatic dressings (QuikClot) for junctional/truncal wounds
- TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of injury if haemorrhagic shock
- Pelvic splint if pelvic injury suspected

**Penetrating Abdominal Injury**
- Cover evisceration with moist dressing
- Do NOT reduce eviscerated bowel
- IV access, nil by mouth, urgent evacuation
- Analgesia

**Eye Injury — Globe Rupture Suspected**
- DO NOT apply pressure to the eye
- Rigid eye shield (not pad)
- Analgesia, antiemetic
- Urgent evacuation — ophthalmological emergency

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Incident Notification**
- All blast-related injuries are notifiable events under WA mines safety legislation
- Notify WA DMIRS and complete site incident report

**Documentation**
- Time of blast, distance from explosion, type of explosive if known
- All symptoms including hearing loss, tinnitus, haemoptysis, abdominal pain
- Initial vital signs, SpO2 trend over time

**Handover to RFDS**
- Emphasise mechanism (proximity to blast, type of explosion)
- Report SpO2 trend — deteriorating SpO2 indicates blast lung
- Report TM status, any haemoptysis
